summary: This advice column installment opens with Aunt Pythia's unsolicited discussion of female orgasm physiology and male objectification before addressing three reader questions: one about accessing student test data to fight VAM teacher evaluations, one about a lonely man over-relying on a female friend for emotional support, and one from a college student doing sex work to pay tuition. The column blends frank sexual discussion with policy critique and practical life advice, maintaining a characteristic irreverence throughout.
key_points: Student test score data used in VAM teacher evaluations is deliberately opaque and legally protected, making data-driven opposition nearly impossible - the fight must be political
The column argues that male genitals are not 'ripe for objectification' the way women's bodies are, reflecting broader gender asymmetries
Lonely people seeking excessive emotional support from one friend need to diversify their social connections before that friendship can be healthy
Sex work's impact on future opportunities depends heavily on identifiability and career goals, but society's prudishness and hostility toward women is the real problem
Educational policy battles around teacher evaluation models are fundamentally about who controls data and uses it as 'an opaque and threatening weapon'
money_quotes: The whole point of it is that they control the data and are using the model as an opaque and threatening weapon.
Once you have a real live woman to talk to in New York I bet she'll be psyched for you and more willing to return your (less frequent and less pathetic) calls.
We are super prudish in the best of times and at less good times we are freaky and hostile towards women. That's not your fault.
Whether it's demeaning is probably something you will not be able to fully answer until you live with the ramifications of having been in it.
I'm hoping you gird yourself for assholes and assumptions, and I hope you can push back.
Making friends is easier than finding a girlfriend and often helps.
key_insight: Whether discussing opaque teacher evaluation algorithms or personal life choices, power asymmetries and societal double standards shape outcomes more than individual merit or data.
